text: Hi, I'm Dr. Lillian Shapiro. There are many reasons to have a hysterectomy.
To some women, keeping your ovaries and or your cervix is a safe option.
Keeping your ovaries will allow your hormones to remain unchanged after your hysterectomy.
The surgeon removes the fallopian tube, carefully excising it to prevent future cancer risks.
We think now that a lot of ovarian cancer actually starts in the fallopian tube.
The surgeon displays the patient's ovary, which appears normal in size, shape, and color.

After this surgery, she will hopefully not be having hot flashes because she still has her ovary.
The surgeon disconnects the uterus at the level of the round ligament, using surgical instruments to separate the ligament from the uterine tissue.
The surgeon proceeds to disconnect the bladder from the uterus after completing the disconnection at the level of the round ligament.
The bladder sits right on top of the uterus, which is one reason that fibroids often cause pressure and frequent urination.
The surgeon visually identifies a fibroid on the uterus, which is the primary cause of the patient's symptoms and the reason for performing the hysterectomy.
The surgeon carefully separates the bladder from the uterus, revealing the fibroid that is causing the patient's symptoms.
The surgeon carefully dissects the tissue at the level of the round ligament, separating the bladder from the uterus.
The surgeon separates the cervix from the fibroid uterus, ensuring the cervix remains intact with the patient while the fibroid uterus is removed.
I have had patients not take any narcotics following their robotic hysterectomy.
Of course, everybody reacts differently.
Patients decide with their nurse if they would like to stay overnight or to go home immediately from the recovery room.
This is not an option for everybody and has to be discussed on an individual basis.
